rationale: Zhou conquered the Shang. Same Han Chinese heartland, continuous ethnic identity.
Qin unified the warring Zhou successor states. Same territory and Han ethnicity.
Han replaced the short-lived Qin on the same Chinese heartland. Same Han ethnicity.
Sui reunified China after centuries of fragmentation. Same Han ethnicity and territory.
Tang replaced the Sui. Same territory and Han ethnicity; Tang founder was Sui royalty.
Khitan (Liao) occupied northern China after Tang collapse. Same northern territory, Khitan (para-Mongolic) ethnicity.
Song reunified the Han Chinese heartland after the Five Dynasties period.
Jurchen (Jin) conquered the Khitan (Liao). Same Manchurian/North China territory, Jurchen ethnicity replaced Khitan.
Mongol conquest of Song China. Same territory, Mongolic ethnicity replaced Han.
Mongols destroyed the Jurchen Jin Dynasty. Same north China/Manchurian territory, Mongol replaced Jurchen.
Han Chinese rebellion expelled the Mongols. Same territory, ethnicity reverted to Han.
Manchu (Jurchen) conquest of Ming China. Same Chinese heartland, Jurchen/Manchu ethnicity replaced Han.
Qing (Manchu) are the direct ethnic successors of the Jurchen Jin. Same Jurchen ethnicity, re-established centuries later.
Silla unified the Korean Peninsula after defeating Goguryeo and Baekje. Same Koreanic ethnicity and continuous territory.
Goryeo succeeded Unified Silla on the same territory with the same Koreanic ethnicity.
Joseon succeeded Goryeo within the same ethnic group and territory, but with a radical shift from Buddhist to Neo-Confucian ideology.
Direct ethnic and territorial continuity within the Japanese people, though the form of government changed multiple times (Heian, Kamakura, Muromachi).
The Meiji Restoration replaced the Shogunate with imperial rule and radical modernization. Same ethnicity and land, but a profound ideological and administrative break.
